segments.s570-g49: بدو is a prepositional/address phrase meaning “to him” and comes first as the indirect object. باغبان گفت is the main clause “the gardener said”; گفت here is the past-tense verb “said” introducing what follows. هرکو بهار is an indefinite relative, “whoever (هرکو) [the] spring (بهار) …”, but the verbal predicate for this clause is postponed to the next segment.
segments.s570-g50: بدیدست is the archaic/perfect verbal form here meaning “has seen” (it supplies the predicate for هرکو بهار: “whoever has seen spring”). سرو is the noun “cypress” and از لب جویبار is a locative phrase “from/at the lip (brink) of the stream,” where از marks origin/location and لب means “brink/edge.” Together: “has seen the cypress at the stream’s edge.”
segments.s570-g51: دو بازو is a simple noun phrase “two arms.” به کردار is a comparative/qualifying construction meaning “in the manner/form of” (به as an adverbial marker + کردار “manner/way”), so به کردار ران هیون describes the arms’ shape: ران (thigh/haunch) of هیون (a beast), i.e. “in the shape of a beast’s haunch.”
segments.s570-g52: برش here functions as a nominal/nominalized idea of appearance/profile (literally “his cut/section/profile”) and is compared with چون بر شیر: چون = “like/as,” بر شیر = “upon/like a lion” (classical comparative phrasing). و connects the next clause. چهرش چو خون = “his face like blood,” where چهرش = his face/appearance and چو = “like/as.” The two parts describe his bearing (lion-like) and the redness of his face (blood-like).
segments.s570-g53: همی is a frequentative/adverb meaning “continually/ever” or a simple presentive particle here (“a… comes”). رنگ شرم literally “the color/complexion of shame” = “a blush/modest flush.” آید is the present tense “comes.” از مهر اوی = “from his love” (از marking cause/origin, مهر = love/affection, اوی = his/her). The clause means: “A blush of modesty comes because of his love.”
segments.s570-g54: Parallel construction to the previous clause: همی (again) + زیب تاج “the beauty/adornment of a crown” + آید “comes.” از چهر اوی = “from his face/countenance” (showing source). The sense is metaphorical: the beauty of a crown seems to come from his countenance.
segments.s570-g9: چو introduces a conditional protasis here (classical/poetic 'if'). مهمان درویش is a nominal phrase 'a guest (who is) a dervish/poor man' placed before the copula; باشی is the 2nd/3rd‑person subjunctive/present form of بودن used in the conditional ('be' / 'are' in 'if you are'). خورش (a cooked dish/stew) is the direct object that will be negated by the next clause.
segments.s570-g10: نیابی is a negative form of یابیدن (‘find/get’) meaning 'you will not find' (classical present/subjunctive reading yields future/gnomic sense). نه functions here as the coordinating negative 'nor' introducing the following list. پوشیدن is the verbal noun 'clothing/to wear' used as an item of provision, و links it to پرورش ('upbringing/care' or 'provision'), so the line lists things the guest will not obtain.
segments.s570-g11: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (dative). گفت here functions as the past saying-verb but the provided lexical senses list did not include the verb use (see note). شاپور is the speaker (postposed subject after the verb, a common poetic order). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and نیک‌بخت is a vocative complement 'fortunate one' (here used as a noun/address).
segments.s570-g12: من این خانه بگزیدم is straightforward SOV in classical style: 'I (من) this house (این خانه) chose (بگزیدم)' — بگزیدم is 1st‑person past of گزیدن 'to choose.' از before تاج و تخت here means 'instead of' / 'over' (showing preference or exclusion) and تاج و تخت is a compound 'crown and throne' (objects contrasted with the chosen house).
segments.s570-g13: یکی is an indefinite 'someone.' زند here is an archaic/poetic present form (from زدن) often used for 'strikes/plays' (e.g. on an instrument) — the supplied sense list does not show that verbal use, so the grammatical note treats it as a poetic verb. و است is the copula construction 'and there is,' followed by آر (wish/desire) as the predicative noun. با برسمت is a prepositional phrase 'with / toward your direction/face' (برسمت = 'toward you'), so the whole clause means 'someone makes a sound and a desire/longing is directed to you.'
segments.s570-g14: به زمزم is a locative phrase 'at/near Zamzam.' یکی here is 'a certain' / 'one (person)' introducing an indefinite person; پاسخی is the noun 'an answer / a question/response' (object), and پرسمت is a first‑person verbal form 'I asked you' (classical/poetic morphology — the provided sense list gives 'I ask you'). Word order is somewhat inverted poetically: locative first, then the event 'a certain one asked you a question/asked for a reply.'
segments.s570-g15: بیاورد is a past tense verb 'he brought/produced.' هرچش (هرچ = هرچه) is the indefinite relative 'whatever/whatever thing.' بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/said' (the construction b- prefix marks an honorific or classical stylistic form) and شاه is the agent 'the king' (postposed). The sequence means 'he brought whatever the king had ordered.'
segments.s570-g16: بیفزود is a past tense verb 'he added.' نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near,' and شه (an archaic/poetic form of شاه) modifies پایگاه ('base / stronghold') giving 'the king's base/stronghold.' Together: 'he added, "near the royal stronghold."' Note the elliptical reported-speech structure: the addition is a new clause introduced by بیفزود.
segments.s570-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیک precedes the noun here as an adverbial 'near/approaching'. درگاه موبد is a genitive phrase 'the mobed's threshold/door' (درگاه + noun). رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached' (here: 'came near/arrived at'). Together they form a temporal clause 'when he came near the mobed's threshold'.
segments.s570-g34: پراگنده modifies گردان and yields 'scattered/dispersed troop(s)' (adjective + noun). و links the two observations. در here means 'door/gate' (not a preposition). بسته is the adjectival past participle 'closed/shut'. دید is the past tense 'saw'. Word order is typical poetic Persian: noun phrase + conjunction + noun + adjective + verb.
segments.s570-g35: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'by/at the sound (with a call)'. زان بارگه = 'from that court' (زان = 'from that', بارگه = 'court'). بار خواست is an idiomatic collocation here: خواست (past 'asked/wanted') takes بار in the sense 'occasion/entrance' — so 'asked for an audience/admission.' This is an older/poetic use of بار with خواست.
segments.s570-g36: چو is 'when'. بگشاد is the poetic past of گشودن 'opened' (here 'the door opened'); در is 'door'. باغبان رفت راست is a small clause: باغبان (the gardener) + رفت (went) + راست as an adverb 'straight/directly' (i.e. he went straight in). Note the prefixed past form بگشاد (ب‑ + گشاد) typical of epic/poetic style.
segments.s570-g37: چو آمد = 'when he came' (چو temporal + آمد past of آمدن). به نزدیک موبد = 'to/near the mobed' (prepositional phrase). فراز here is an adverb/particle meaning 'up/forward, before (someone)' — it prepares the following action of holding up or presenting something to the mobed.
segments.s570-g38: بدو = 'to him'. مهر بنمود is 'he showed the seal' (مهر = seal/amulet; بنمود is the past of نمودن, poetic 'showed/held up'). و links the clauses. بردش نماز is idiomatic: برد + ش (him) + نماز — here best read as 'he offered/said his prayer (before him)' or 'presented his petition/prayer'; the supplied senses list did not include a direct match for this verbal use of بردن, so the precise sense is represented separately.
segments.s570-g39: چو introduces the temporal clause 'when'. موبد نگه کرد uses نگه کرد (literally 'made a gaze') as a verbal idiom 'looked, fixed his gaze'. و connects the action of looking to the resulting perception. آن مهره is 'that amulet/seal' and دید is the past 'saw'.
segments.s570-g40: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' (classical form of از). شادی دل = 'the heart from joy' or 'with joy'; رای‌زن here denotes 'the counselor/one who gives counsel'. بردمید is an older/poetic past form meaning 'was lifted/took flight' or 'was carried away' — idiomatically 'the heart leapt' (i.e. he was filled with joy). The whole phrase means 'the counselor's heart leapt with joy.'
segments.s570-g17: به زمزم بدو گفت برگوی راست is an utterance introduction: به + name marks the addressee ('to Zamzam'); بدو is an indirect object pronoun 'to him' (reiterating or pointing to the person); گفت is the past verb 'said' (here used to introduce direct speech); برگوی is the archaic/poetic imperative of برگو 'say, tell'; راست is an adverb/adjective 'true/truthfully' modifying the imperative. Word order is typical poetic/Persian: prepositional phrase + indirect object + verb, then the imperative phrase as reported speech.
segments.s570-g18: This clause is the quoted question. کجا is the interrogative 'where' placed up front for emphasis; موبد موبد is a vocative/doubled address (repetition for emphasis or calling the priest); اکنون 'now' locates the time; کجاست is the fused interrogative+copula ('where is ...') — in this poetic line the copula appears as ست. The overall order is interrogative + vocative + temporal + copula.
segments.s570-g19: چنین داد پاسخ ورا باغبان is a compact SOV/Persian expression: چنین 'thus' introduces the manner; داد 'gave' is the past tense verb used with پاسخ 'answer' (پاسخ داد = 'answered'); ورا 'to him' marks the recipient; باغبان at the end is the subject 'the gardener' (subject following the verb is common in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s570-g20: که‌ای is an exclamatory/vocative structure (که + ای) meaning roughly 'O who...' or 'Who are you—' addressed to someone; پاک‌دل is a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted' modifying مرد 'man' (the head noun); شیرین‌زبان is a descriptive compound 'sweet‑tongued' (an epithet). Together they form the gardener's direct address to the addressee.
segments.s570-g21: دو چشمم is 'my two eyes' with the possessive suffix -م on چشم; ز جایی که 'from the place where' is a prepositional relativizer ('from the place that...'); دارم is the present auxiliary 'I have/am (doing)' used here with the following verbal noun/verb to form a continuous sense; نشست is used as the verb 'sit'/'am sitting' (here it functions as the action location). The phrase therefore means 'from where I am sitting'.
segments.s570-g22: بدان is a fused prepositional+demonstrative ('to/into that' or 'into that'); خانه موبدان is a genitive construction 'the house of the mobeds' (موبدان = plural of موبد); موبه‌دست (here shown as موبه دست) is an adverbial phrase meaning 'exactly/precisely at hand' or 'within reach' (موبه used as intensifier/adverb 'exactly' + دست 'hand' → 'at hand'). Word order places the locative/goal phrase after the clause in poetic style.
segments.s570-g23: نهانی 'secretly' is an adverb modifying the clause; به پالیزبان 'to the gardener' marks the addressee with the preposition به; گفت is the past verb 'said' introducing reported speech (here the list of senses provided did not include the verbal reading, so that sense is indicated below as absent); شاه 'the king' is the subject — in poetic order the verb precedes the subject.
segments.s570-g24: که introduces indirect speech 'that/that you should...'; از مهتر 'from the chief/headman' marks the source; ده is the numeral 'ten' (present in the running text of the sentence though the provided word list omitted it) qualifying گل مهره 'flower‑like beads' or 'beads/pearls'; خواه here is a verbal/formal request ('ask/seek' or 'desire') — in older/poetic Persian خواه can function to express a request or desire ('ask for/seek'). The overall meaning is an instruction to request ten beads from the chief.
segments.s570-g1: ببود is an archaic/past form of 'بود' meaning 'it was'. آن شب is a time adverbial ('that night'). The sequence و خورد و گفت و شنید links three past actions with و (and): خورد (past of خوردن, 'ate'), گفت (past of گفتن, 'spoke/said'), شنید (past of شنیدن, 'heard/listened'). The subject is omitted (typical in Persian) and the verbs describe what happened during that night.
segments.s570-g2: سپیده is the subject 'dawn / first light'. چو here means 'when'. از کوه is a prepositional phrase 'from the mountain'. سر بر کشید is an idiomatic verbal phrase: سر (head/top) + بر کشیدن ('to raise/lift up'); کشید is past tense. The clause means 'when dawn lifted its head from the mountain' with the temporal clause introduced by چو.
segments.s570-g3: چو = 'when'. زرین درفشی is an adjective-noun phrase with زرین ('golden') qualifying درفشی ('banner/standard'). برآورد is a past verbal form meaning 'raised/brought forth' (poetic inversion is frequent). راغ appears at the end as the agent/subject (either a proper name or 'the desirous one'); Persian poetry often places the subject after the verb.
segments.s570-g4: This line is inverted: خداوند باغ ('the lord/master of the garden') is the subject. بر میهمان شد is a compound predicate: بر here marks direction/relationship ('to/for'), میهمان ('guest') is the role taken, and شد is the past of شدن 'became'. Literally: 'the lord of the garden became (a) guest (to him)'.
segments.s570-g5: بدو is a prepositional/clitic phrase 'to him' (به + او contracted), and گفت is the past 'said'. روز تو فرخنده باد is reported speech using باد as an optative particle meaning 'may ... be' (a wish): روزِ تو ('your day') + فرخنده ('auspicious') + باد ('may it be').
segments.s570-g6: سرت is 'your head' (سَر + enclitic ت), برتر از is the comparative 'higher/superior than' (برتر + از). بر بارنده is a noun phrase where بارنده is a participial adjective 'bringing/bringing rain' (i.e. 'rain‑bringer'); the intervening بر is part of the idiomatic expression here. The clause ends with باد (optative 'may'), so the whole is a wish: 'May your head be loftier than the rain‑bringer's.'
segments.s570-g7: سزای is the genitive/construct form 'the recompense/desert of'. تومان is the genitive noun that follows ('tuman' — here likely 'a large sum' or a unit of value). جایگاهی نبود is a nominal predicate: جایگاهی ('a place / position') + نبود (past negative of بودن 'there was not'). The clause says there was no place fitting for that recompense.
segments.s570-g8: به is the preposition 'for' / 'to'; آرام is an adjective 'calm/quiet'; شایسته is another adjective 'worthy/appropriate' modifying گاهی ('a time / occasion' or 'a place' in poetic usage). نبود is the past negative 'there was not'. Together: 'there was no suitably calm/appropriate time/place.'
segments.s570-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. بشنید is a past tense verb 'he heard'. ز + و here represent the preposition ز 'from' plus a clitic pronoun (spelled separately in the segmentation); together they mean 'from him'. این سخن باغبان is the object 'this speech/words of the gardener' placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s570-g26: A string of coordinated nouns (گل و مشک) linked by و 'and', followed by the past-tense desiderative می‌خواست 'he wanted/desired' (here used as a past/habitual desire). The final clause و آمد دمان places آمد 'came' in the past; دمان functions as a temporal/locational word (poetic usage) indicating timing or arrival.
segments.s570-g27: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'. بنهاد is the past/telic verb 'placed/put' (بنهاد = بناهاد/نهاد). بر introduces the prepositional phrase 'on'; نگین is the direct object 'a gem' governed by بر گل ('on the flower'). Word order here is poetic but straightforward: subject — verb — prepositional phrase.
segments.s570-g28: بدان is functioning as a directional/demonstrative 'to that' (to/for that person) followed by باغبان 'gardener' as indirect object. داد is the past verb 'gave'. کرد + آفرین is a light-verb construction where کرد 'did/made' takes آفرین 'bravo/exclamation' — together 'praised/exclaimed "bravo"'.
segments.s570-g29: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (ب + او). گفت here is the speech-verb 'said' (past); کین is an archaic/demonstrative 'this' (poetic), introducing گل 'flower' as the object. به موبد سپار: به 'to' plus موبد 'the mobed/priest', and سپار is the imperative 'entrust/hand over'.
segments.s570-g30: نگر is an imperative 'look/see' (observe), followed by تا introducing purpose/result 'so that' (or 'to see what...'). چه گوید is an embedded clause 'what he may say' (گوید is a present/subjunctive-form verb here). همه گوش دار is an idiomatic instruction 'everyone, give ear' — گوش used metonymically for 'attention', and دار is part of the periphrastic imperative 'have/listen'.
segments.s570-g31: سپیده دمان is a temporal compound meaning 'at dawn/at daybreak' (سپیده + دمان). مرد is the subject 'the man'; با مهر شاه is an adjunct 'with the king's favor' — با 'with' plus مهر used as 'favor/kindness' and شاه as its possessor. The whole phrase describes who set out at dawn.
segments.s570-g32: بر موبد is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the mobed' (بر used as 'to/toward' in classical/poetic Persian). The repetition موبد موبد is a poetic apposition/inversion: the first occurrence completes the 'to' phrase, the second is the subject 'the mobed' who آمد 'came' — پگاه again marks early morning/dawn (picking out time of arrival).
segments.s570-g41: وزان پس بران نام چندی گریست is a compact temporal clause: وزان پس (و + از + آن پس, 'from that time on') sets the time; بران (بِ + آن) introduces the object 'on/for that'; نام is the object 'name'; چندی qualifies the duration 'for a while'; گریست is the past tense verb 'he wept'. Word order is topic/time → object → duration → verb, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s570-g42: بدان باغبان گفت کاین مهر کیست is a reporting clause. بدان is the dative 'to that' (بِ + دان) pointing to the gardener; باغبان is the indirect object; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing the quoted question; کاین (که + این) is a demonstrative 'that/this'; مهر is the substantive 'love/affection' (here rhetorical = 'beloved'); کی ست is the archaic inversion of the copula + interrogative 'who is'.
segments.s570-g43: چنین داد پاسخ که‌ای نامدار is an introductory reported reply: چنین داد پاسخ literally 'he gave such a reply' (داد = past 'gave'); که‌ای here functions as the start of the quoted address/quote ('that: O ...') with ای used vocatively; نامدار is the vocative adjective 'renowned'.
segments.s570-g44: نشسته به خان منست این سوار is the content of the reply. نشسته is a past participle/adjectival perfect 'seated' (describing state); به خان is a locative phrase 'in/at the khan' (به here = 'in/at'); منست appears as an archaic/contracted form meaning roughly 'belongs to me / is of my house' (here indicating proprietary/association) or generically 'is (mine)'; این سوار is the demonstrative subject 'this rider'. The sentence places the predicate participle before the subject, a common poetic word order.
segments.s570-g45: یکی ماه با وی چو سرو سهی is a descriptive nominal phrase. یکی + noun ('a certain ...') introduces an individual; ماه is a poetic epithet 'moon' = beautiful/round face; با وی 'with him' places this person beside the rider; چو 'like' introduces a simile, and سرو سهی 'a dark/black cypress' is the simile's vehicle. The phrase omits an explicit verb (typical in descriptive lines) or implies 'was'.
segments.s570-g46: خردمند و با زیب و با فرهی lists attributes: خردمند 'wise' is an adjective; the repeated با 'with' before زیب and فرهی is a poetic coordinate construction meaning 'endowed with/possessing beauty and nobility'. The simple conjunction و links the qualities.
segments.s570-g47: بدو گفت موبد که‌ای نامجوی is the reporting clause: بدو 'to him' (بِ + ضمِیر) marks the indirect object/recipient; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (reporting speech); موبد is the speaker 'the mobed/priest'; که‌ای begins the quoted address 'O ...' and نامجوی is the vocative 'name-seeker' (one asking a name or seeking names).
segments.s570-g48: نشان که دارد به بالا و روی is the interrogative content: نشان 'a mark/sign' is the direct object; که دارد 'who has' uses دارد (present/past-habitual 'has/possesses') with که as the interrogative 'who'; به بالا و روی 'on the brow and face' is a locative phrase (به + noun), listing two places. Grammatically it is a simple relative/interrogative clause asking whose mark it is and where it is borne.

sentences.s570-s25: The gardener said to him: Whoever has seen spring has seen the cypress at the edge of the stream.
sentences.s570-s26: His two arms, in shape like the haunchs of a beast; his bearing like a lion’s, and his face like blood.
sentences.s570-s27: A blush of modesty comes from his love; from his countenance the beauty of a crown appears.
sentences.s570-s5: If you are a poor/dervish guest, you will find no cooked dish, nor clothing or care.
sentences.s570-s6: Shapur said to him, 'O fortunate one, I chose this house instead of crown and throne.'
sentences.s570-s7: Someone strikes (or performs) and a longing is directed toward you; at Zamzam a certain person put a question to you.
sentences.s570-s8: He brought whatever the king commanded; the king added, 'near the royal stronghold.'
sentences.s570-s17: When he came close to the mobed's threshold, he saw the scattered troops and the door shut.
sentences.s570-s18: At the sound from that court he asked for admission; when the door opened, the gardener went straight in.
sentences.s570-s19: When he came close to the mobed, he showed the seal to him and offered his prayer.
sentences.s570-s20: When the mobed looked and saw that amulet, the counselor's heart leapt with joy.
sentences.s570-s9: He said to Zamzam, "Tell the truth: where is the mobed now?"
sentences.s570-s10: Thus the gardener answered him, "O pure‑hearted, sweet‑tongued man!"
sentences.s570-s11: From the place where I sit, my two eyes look into that house of the mobeds, right at hand.
sentences.s570-s12: The king secretly said to the gardener, "Ask the chief for ten beads."
sentences.s570-s1: It was that night, and he ate and spoke and listened; when dawn lifted its head from the mountain.
sentences.s570-s2: When Rāgh raised a golden banner, the lord of the garden became his guest.
sentences.s570-s3: He said to him, 'May your day be auspicious; may your head be loftier than that of the rain-bringer.'
sentences.s570-s4: There was no place worthy of the reward of the tuman; there was no suitably peaceful place.
sentences.s570-s13: When he heard these words from the gardener, he desired flowers, musk, and wine, and came at once.
sentences.s570-s14: The sovereign placed a gem on the flower; he gave it to that gardener and cried 'Bravo'.
sentences.s570-s15: He said to him, 'Entrust this flower to the mobed; see what he says — everyone listen.'
sentences.s570-s16: At dawn the man favored by the king went to the mobed; the mobed came at daybreak.
sentences.s570-s21: After that he wept for that name for a while; he said to the gardener, "Who is this love?"
sentences.s570-s22: He replied thus: "O renowned one, this rider is seated in my khan."
sentences.s570-s23: A certain moonlike one was with him, like a dark cypress — wise, and with beauty and with nobility.
sentences.s570-s24: To him the mobed said: "O name-seeker, who bears a mark on brow and face?"
